summary: Hotz argues that money is merely a map attempting to represent value, not value itself, and that this map is becoming increasingly divorced from reality. He critiques both capitalism and communism as flawed systems—capitalism for enabling rent-seeking and communism for misallocating capital to poor deployers. The post responds to criticism of his previous writing by doubling down on the idea that when capital becomes too concentrated or disconnected from real growth, the system breaks. His solution is 'acceleration'—focusing on growing the pie rather than fighting over its distribution, with capital going to those who can most effectively deploy it for real economic growth.
key_points: Money is a map of value, not value itself—and a map that doesn't accurately capture territory becomes useless
When one entity accumulates all money, it becomes worthless, spelling the logical end of capitalism
Risk-free returns above real economic growth (e.g., 5% money market vs 3% growth) mean someone else is losing
Both capitalism and communism fail: capitalism rewards rent-seekers who protect moats, communism gives to poor capital deployers
The focus should be on growing the pie (acceleration) rather than fighting over distribution
money_quotes: Money is a map, it is not a territory.
You can erase the mountains from the map, but you still have to climb over them in real life, and even worse, now you don't have a map!
A map that doesn't accurately capture a territory is not a Schelling point.
Many capitalists I meet are as stupid as communists, trying to give a moral justification for their system.
A economic system is only good in so much as it effectively deploys capital for real growth.
If real economic growth is only 3 percent, any time you are earning beyond that, somebody else is losing.
Stop worrying so much about the distribution of the pie. Start thinking about how to make the pie bigger.
From each according to his ability, to each according to his ability to effectively deploy capital to achieve real economic growth.
key_insight: Economic systems should be judged solely by their ability to deploy capital for real growth, not by moral justifications or distributional concerns.
